title: Cauliflower cheese
subtitle: Proper béchamel, mature cheddar, browned breadcrumb top.
excerpt: A whole cauliflower blanched until just tender, blanketed in a thick, sharp cheddar sauce and baked until the top blisters and the sauce bubbles at the edges. A Sunday side that often steals the plate.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — cauliflower with béchamel sauce in the vegetable chapter. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg (cauliflower with cream sauce). The breadcrumb topping is traditional British kitchen practice documented through mid-twentieth-century domestic cookery guides.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the cheese sauce up to two days ahead and keep refrigerated. Blanch the cauliflower the morning of. Assemble cold and bake from cold — add five minutes to the oven time.

content.text: Cauliflower cheese is built on a béchamel (butter, flour, milk) enriched with enough mature cheddar to make it almost aggressive. The cauliflower should be tender all the way through before it goes in the dish; the oven has to brown the top, not cook the vegetable. Undercooked cauliflower in baked cheese sauce is the dish's single most common failure.

The breadcrumb topping is not essential but it earns its place: mixed with a little extra cheese, it catches the heat and sets into a layer that snaps under the spoon. Without it the surface is smooth and uniform, slightly institutional. The crumb takes ten seconds to mix and changes the texture of every mouthful.
A large, shallow baking dish shows off the cauliflower properly and lets the topping brown evenly. A deep dish traps steam and the top takes longer to colour.

Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil. Add {{cauliflower}} cauliflower florets and simmer for 6 to 8 minutes until completely tender — a skewer goes in with no resistance. Drain well in a colander and leave to steam for two minutes. While the cauliflower steams, heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan) and butter the baking dish.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir constantly for two minutes to cook out the raw taste; the mixture will look sandy. Take the pan off the heat. Pour in {{whole-milk}} of warm milk gradually, whisking hard after each addition to keep the sauce smooth. Return to medium heat and stir continuously until the sauce thickens to the consistency of thick double cream, about five minutes.

Remove from the heat. Add 90 g of the {{cheddar}} of grated mature cheddar, the {{english-mustard-powder}} of mustard powder, a few gratings of nutmeg, {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt, and {{white-pepper}} of white pepper. Stir until the cheese melts completely. Taste: the sauce should be assertive and well-seasoned, because the cauliflower will dilute it slightly in the oven.

Arrange the cauliflower florets in the buttered baking dish. Pour the cheese sauce evenly over and around them — push the florets down gently so the sauce reaches the base. Mix the remaining 30 g of cheddar with the breadcrumbs if using and scatter over the top. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the top is deep golden and the sauce is bubbling at the sides.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Most issues come from the sauce or the blanching, rarely from the oven.

items.symptom: Cauliflower still firm in the centre after baking
items.cause: Not blanched long enough before it went in the dish.
items.fix: Blanch until a skewer meets no resistance. The oven only browns the dish, it does not cook the vegetable through from raw.

items.cause: Hot milk added too fast, or the roux wasn't smooth before milk was added.
items.fix: Whisk off the heat between additions of milk. If lumps form, strain through a fine sieve and return to the heat.

items.cause: Too much heat after the cheese was added, or too much stirring.
items.fix: Add cheese off the heat or at the lowest simmer. Stir to melt, then stop.

items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or dish covered with foil.
items.fix: Bake uncovered at a full 200°C fan. If needed, switch to the grill for two to three minutes at the end.

content.text: Cauliflower cheese sits at the boundary between side dish and main course. On a Sunday roast table it accompanies the meat; on a weekday it stands alone with a green salad. Mrs Beeton documented cauliflower with cream sauce in 1861 as a refined vegetable dish for the dinner table; by the mid twentieth century it had become a staple of the British school dinner and the institutional kitchen. The sharp cheddar version with mustard is the domestic improvement on that institutional memory, the one that makes people glad to see it arrive at the table.
